title: Context-Driven Incremental Compression for Multi-Turn Dialogue Generation
authors: Yeongseo Jung, Jaehyeok Kim, Eunseo Jung, Jiachuan Wang, Yongqi Zhang, Ka Chun Cheung, Simon See, Lei Chen
summary:
**Improving Conversational AI: A New Approach to Efficient and Accurate Dialogue Generation**

Imagine having a conversation with a chatbot or virtual assistant that can understand and respond to you over many turns, without getting confused or losing track of what you've discussed. Researchers have made a significant step towards making this a reality by developing a new approach called Context-Driven Incremental Compression (C-DIC).

The challenge with current conversational AI systems is that they rely on a growing amount of dialogue history to generate responses, which can become inefficient and prone to errors. Existing solutions, such as truncating or summarizing the conversation, can lead to loss of important information.

C-DIC addresses this issue by treating a conversation as a series of interconnected threads and storing a compact summary of each thread in a shared memory. This allows the system to efficiently retrieve and update relevant information across turns, reducing errors and improving performance.

The researchers tested C-DIC on long-form dialogue benchmarks and found that it outperformed existing methods in terms of efficiency and accuracy. Notably, C-DIC maintained stable performance over hundreds of dialogue turns, making it a promising approach for developing high-quality conversational AI systems.

This breakthrough has the potential to enable more natural and engaging conversations with chatbots and virtual assistants, with applications in areas such as customer service, language learning, and healthcare.

title: FACTR 2: Learning External Force Sensing for Commodity Robot Arms Improves Policy Learning
authors: Steven Oh, Jason Jingzhou Liu, Tony Tao, Philip Han, Kenneth Shaw, Satoshi Funabashi, Ruslan Salakhutdinov, Deepak Pathak
summary:
**Making Robots Smarter: A New Way to Sense Forces**

Imagine having a robot arm that can perform delicate tasks, like assembling furniture or handling fragile objects. To do this, the robot needs to sense the forces it applies to objects, but many robot arms don't have the necessary sensors because they are expensive.

Researchers have developed a new method called Neural External Torque Estimation (NEXT) that allows robot arms to estimate external forces without needing special sensors. NEXT learns to estimate these forces by analyzing just 10 minutes of data collected while the robot arm moves freely. Surprisingly, NEXT's estimates are as good as those from dedicated force sensors.

The researchers also created a new training method called Force-Informed Re-Sampling Training (FIRST), which helps robots learn policies (or strategies) for performing tasks more effectively. By emphasizing the moments when the robot arm makes contact with objects, FIRST enables robots to learn better policies.

Together, NEXT and FIRST enable low-cost robot arms to perform tasks that require sensing forces, such as teleoperation (controlling a robot remotely) and complex manipulation tasks. In tests, robots using NEXT and FIRST outperformed previous methods by over 17% in task progress. This breakthrough brings us closer to having affordable, capable robots that can assist us in various tasks.

title: Redesign Mixture-of-Experts Routers with Manifold Power Iteration
authors: Songhao Wu, Ang Lv, Ruobing Xie, Yankai Lin
summary:
Here's a summary of the research paper for a general audience:

**Improving AI Models with a New Router Design**

Artificial intelligence (AI) models are getting bigger and more complex, with some having billions of parameters. One type of model, called Mixture-of-Experts (MoE), uses a special component called a "router" to decide which parts of the model (called "experts") to activate for a given task. The router's job is to figure out which experts are most relevant to a particular task.

The researchers in this study propose a new way to design the router, called Manifold Power Iteration (MPI). Their approach helps the router better understand the relationships between the experts and the tasks, making the MoE model more effective. The researchers tested their new router design on large MoE models with up to 11 billion parameters and found that it improved their performance.

In simple terms, the researchers found a way to help AI models make better decisions about which parts of the model to use for a given task, leading to more efficient and effective models. This could have significant implications for a wide range of applications, from natural language processing to computer vision.

title: ATLAS: Active Theory Learning for Automated Science
authors: Noémi Éltető, Nathaniel D. Daw, Kimberly L. Stachenfeld, Kevin J. Miller
summary:
Here's a summary of the research paper "ATLAS: Active Theory Learning for Automated Science" for a general audience:

**Automating Scientific Discovery**

Imagine being able to accelerate scientific breakthroughs by automating the process of designing experiments. Researchers have developed a new framework called ATLAS (Active Theory Learning for Automated Science) that uses artificial intelligence to do just that. ATLAS is designed to help scientists discover new insights by automatically generating hypotheses and designing experiments to test them.

**How it Works**

ATLAS uses a combination of machine learning and experimental design to identify the most informative experiments to run. It generates a range of possible explanations for a phenomenon and then designs experiments that can help distinguish between them. This approach allows scientists to gather data more efficiently and gain a deeper understanding of complex systems.

**Test Results**

In a test case involving artificial agents that learn through trial and error, ATLAS was able to design experiments that led to a 5-10 times improvement in the efficiency of data collection compared to random experimentation. The results were also validated against experiments designed by human experts, demonstrating the potential of ATLAS to accelerate scientific discovery.

**Implications**

The development of ATLAS has significant implications for fields such as cognitive science, where understanding complex behaviors is crucial. By automating the process of experimental design, ATLAS can help scientists gain new insights and make breakthroughs more quickly. The framework has the potential to be applied to a wide range of scientific domains, from biology and psychology to physics and engineering.

title: APPO: Agentic Procedural Policy Optimization
authors: Xucong Wang, Ziyu Ma, Yong Wang, Yuxiang Ji, Shidong Yang, Guanhua Chen, Pengkun Wang, Xiangxiang Chu
summary:
**Improving AI Decision-Making: A New Approach**

Researchers have made significant progress in developing AI agents that can use tools to achieve complex goals. However, a major challenge remains: understanding which decisions made by the AI agent lead to successful outcomes. Current methods often rely on rough estimates, making it difficult to pinpoint exactly which intermediate decisions are crucial.

To address this issue, the researchers proposed a new approach called Agentic Procedural Policy Optimization (APPO). APPO focuses on two key aspects: identifying the most important decision points in a sequence and assigning credit to those decisions. Unlike previous methods, APPO breaks down the decision-making process into finer, more detailed steps.

The researchers tested APPO on 13 different benchmarks and found that it consistently outperformed existing methods, achieving nearly a 4-point improvement. Notably, APPO maintained efficient tool usage and provided interpretable results, making it a promising approach for developing more effective AI agents. This work has the potential to lead to more informed and targeted decision-making in AI systems.

title: UniIntervene: Agentic Intervention for Efficient Real-World Reinforcement Learning
authors: Haoyuan Deng, Yitong Gao, Yudong Lin, Haichao Liu, Zhenyu Wu, Ziwei Wang
summary:
**Breakthrough in Robot Learning: UniIntervene Reduces Need for Human Intervention**

Imagine a robot learning to perform tasks on its own, but sometimes getting stuck or making mistakes. Currently, humans have to intervene frequently to correct the robot, which can be time-consuming and costly. Researchers have developed a new system called UniIntervene, which enables robots to learn more efficiently and reduce the need for human intervention.

UniIntervene works by detecting when the robot is getting stuck or exploring unproductive paths. When this happens, the system takes over and guides the robot towards more promising actions, using a kind of "memory" of past successful interventions. This approach allows the robot to learn from its mistakes and recover more quickly, reducing the need for human corrections.

In experiments, UniIntervene improved the success rate of robots performing various tasks by 8.6%, while reducing the number of human interventions by 57%. This breakthrough has significant implications for the development of robots that can learn and adapt in real-world settings, with potential applications in areas such as manufacturing, healthcare, and logistics. By making robot learning more efficient and autonomous, UniIntervene brings us closer to a future where robots can learn and improve on their own, with minimal human intervention.

title: Breaking Entropy Bounds: Accelerating RL Training via MTP with Rejection Sampling
authors: Yucheng Li, Huiqiang Jiang, Yang Xu, Jianxin Yang, Yi Zhang, Yizhong Cao, Yuhao Shen, Fan Zhou, Rui Men, Jianwei Zhang, An Yang, Bowen Yu, Bo Zheng, Fei Huang, Junyang Lin, Dayiheng Liu, Jingren Zhou
summary:
**Breaking Through Speed Limits in AI Training**

Imagine training a highly intelligent AI model to perform complex tasks like generating text, solving math problems, or writing code. A major bottleneck in this process is the "rollout stage," where the model generates text or other outputs to learn from its mistakes. Researchers have proposed a technique called Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) to speed up this process, but its effectiveness decreases over time.

A team of researchers has investigated this issue and developed a new approach called Bebop. They found that the MTP technique's performance is limited by the model's "entropy," or uncertainty, which increases during training. To overcome this, they introduced a new method called probabilistic rejection sampling, which helps to mitigate the impact of entropy.

The researchers also developed a novel training objective, called TV loss, which directly optimizes the acceptance rate of MTP. This resulted in significant improvements, achieving up to 95% acceptance rates and 25% extra inference throughput gains. They tested various online MTP training strategies and found that pre-training with TV loss and rejection sampling leads to consistent performance throughout the training process.

The Bebop approach achieved up to 1.8x end-to-end acceleration in async RL training of large language models, making it a significant breakthrough in AI training. This research provides practical recipes for integrating MTP into large-scale RL pipelines, paving the way for faster and more efficient AI training.

title: On Subquadratic Architectures: From Applications to Principles
authors: Anamaria-Roberta Hartl, Levente Zólyomi, David Stap, Pieter-Jan Hoedt, Niklas Schmidinger, Lukas Hauzenberger, Sebastian Böck, Günter Klambauer, Sepp Hochreiter
summary:
**Breakthrough in Sequence Modeling: A More Efficient and Effective Approach**

Researchers have made a significant advancement in sequence modeling, a crucial aspect of artificial intelligence that enables computers to understand and process sequential data, such as text, code, or time-series data. The dominant approach, Transformers, has a major drawback: its "quadratic attention" mechanism becomes extremely computationally expensive as the sequence length increases.

To address this limitation, researchers explored alternative "subquadratic" architectures, which are more scalable and efficient. They compared three leading approaches: xLSTM, Mamba-2, and Gated DeltaNet. The study found that xLSTM outperformed the others in tasks that require complex dependencies, such as:

1. **Code understanding**: xLSTM excelled in pre-training code models, which involves learning to comprehend and generate code.
2. **Knowledge distillation**: xLSTM effectively distilled knowledge from large language models to smaller code models.
3. **Time-series analysis**: xLSTM performed well in pre-training foundation models for time-series data, which is essential for applications like forecasting and anomaly detection.

To understand why xLSTM excelled, researchers analyzed its underlying architecture and identified two key factors:

1. **State tracking**: xLSTM's ability to track and accumulate information over time allowed it to capture complex dependencies more effectively.
2. **Memory dynamics**: xLSTM's gating scheme enabled more flexible and stable memory correction, which contributed to its superior performance.

These findings have significant implications for the development of more efficient and effective sequence models, which can be applied to a wide range of applications, from natural language processing to time-series analysis.

title: Latent World Recovery for Multimodal Learning with Missing Modalities
authors: Hui Wang, Tianyu Ren, Joseph Butler, Christopher Baker, Karen Rafferty, Simon McDade
summary:
**Recovering Hidden Patterns in Multimodal Data**

Imagine you're trying to diagnose a disease, and you have different types of data about a patient, such as genetic information, medical images, and lab results. But often, not all of this data is available, which can make it harder to make accurate predictions. Researchers have developed a new framework called Latent World Recovery (LWR) to tackle this problem.

LWR works by creating a shared "map" of the different types of data, even if some of them are missing. This map helps the framework to combine the available data in a way that's useful for making predictions. Unlike other methods, LWR doesn't try to guess the missing data or require that all data be present. Instead, it uses the available data to make the best possible predictions.

The researchers tested LWR on real-world data from biology and medicine, and found that it was effective in tasks such as predicting cancer outcomes. This approach has the potential to improve our ability to make accurate predictions when working with incomplete data, which is a common problem in many fields. By recovering hidden patterns in multimodal data, LWR could lead to better diagnosis and treatment of diseases.

title: Anatomy of Post-Training: Using Interpretability to Characterize Data and Shape the Learning Signal
authors: Leon Bergen, Usha Bhalla, Sidharth Baskaran, Max Loeffler, Raphael Sarfati, Dhruvil Gala, Ryan Panwar, Santiago Aranguri, Thomas Fel, Atticus Geiger, Matthew Kowal, Siddharth Boppana, Daniel Balsam, Owen Lewis, Jack Merullo, Thomas McGrath, Ekdeep Singh Lubana
summary:
**Improving AI Training: A New Approach to Shaping Model Behavior**

Imagine training a language model to generate human-like text. The goal is to teach the model what makes good text and what doesn't. Currently, this process involves optimizing a simple score that summarizes what makes good text, but this approach can lead to problems. For example, the model might learn to mimic certain styles or phrases without truly understanding their meaning, or it might become overly obedient to certain prompts.

Researchers have proposed a new approach to improve this process. They suggest using interpretability techniques to analyze the data used to train the model and identify specific concepts or behaviors that the model should learn. This allows for more fine-grained control over the model's learning process and helps to mitigate undesirable behaviors.

The researchers developed a pipeline that uses interpretability protocols to analyze the data and develop statistical hypotheses about what makes good text. They then used this information to shape the model's learning signal, or the data used to train the model. The results showed that this approach can help diagnose and mitigate problems with existing training data, and can also be used to amplify desired properties such as safeguards and model personality.

Overall, this research suggests that interpretability can be a powerful tool for improving AI training. By providing a more detailed understanding of what the model is learning, researchers can create more effective and responsible AI systems.

title: Atlas H&E-TME: Scalable AI-Based Tissue Profiling at Expert Pathologist-Level Accuracy
authors: Kai Standvoss, Miriam Hägele, Rosemarie Krupar, Julika Ribbat-Idel, Jennifer Altschüler, Gerrit Erdmann, Hans Pinckaers, Evelyn Ramberger, Madleen Drinkwitz, Ádám Nárai, Alexander Möllers, Katja Lingelbach, Sebastian Kons, Lukas Hönig, Recepcan Adigüzel, Joana Baião, Alberto Megina Gonzalo, Marius Teodorescu, Marie-Lisa Eich, Paolo Chetta, Shakil Merchant, Verena Aumiller, Simon Schallenberg, Andrew Norgan, Klaus-Robert Müller, Lukas Ruff, Maximilian Alber, Frederick Klauschen
summary:
**Breakthrough in Cancer Research: AI System Analyzes Tissue Samples with Expert-Level Accuracy**

Imagine a tool that can help doctors analyze tissue samples from cancer patients more accurately and efficiently. Researchers have developed an artificial intelligence (AI) system called Atlas H&E-TME, which can analyze tissue samples stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), a common technique used in pathology.

The AI system can examine entire tissue samples, identifying different types of cells and tissue regions, and providing over 4,500 detailed readouts per sample. This is a significant improvement over traditional methods, which are often time-consuming and rely on human pathologists' interpretations.

To validate the accuracy of Atlas H&E-TME, the researchers used a dual validation framework. They compared the AI system's results with those of human pathologists and also used a more informed reference, immunohistochemistry (IHC), to validate the results. IHC is a technique that uses antibodies to detect specific proteins in tissue samples.

The results showed that Atlas H&E-TME matches or exceeds the performance of human pathologists working with H&E-stained samples alone. The AI system was tested on over 200,000 high-confidence annotations across 1,500+ cases, spanning eight cancer types and their metastatic sites. This extensive testing demonstrates the system's robustness and generalizability across a broad range of morphological and technical variations.

The development of Atlas H&E-TME has significant implications for cancer research and treatment. By turning H&E-stained tissue samples into a scalable, quantitative window into the tumor and its microenvironment, this AI system lays the foundation for the next generation of tissue-based biomarkers. These biomarkers can help doctors better understand cancer biology, identify new therapeutic targets, and develop more effective treatments.

**In Simple Terms:** This AI system helps doctors analyze tissue samples from cancer patients more accurately and efficiently. It can identify different types of cells and tissue regions, providing detailed information that can help doctors better understand cancer and develop more effective treatments.

title: Claw-SWE-Bench: A Benchmark for Evaluating OpenClaw-style Agent Harnesses on Coding Tasks
authors: Mengyu Zheng, Kai Han, Boxun Li, Haiyang Xu, Yuchuan Tian, Wei He, Hang Zhou, Jianyuan Guo, Hailin Hu, Lin Ma, Chao Xu, Guohao Dai, Lixue Xia, Yunchao Wei, Yunhe Wang, Yu Wang
summary:
**Improving AI Coding Assistants: A New Benchmark for Evaluation**

Researchers have developed a new benchmark called Claw-SWE-Bench to evaluate the coding abilities of AI agents, such as OpenClaw, that can perform tasks autonomously. These agents are becoming increasingly popular, but it's challenging to measure their coding skills accurately.

The Claw-SWE-Bench benchmark provides a fair and standardized way to compare different AI agents on coding tasks. It consists of 350 coding challenges across 8 languages and 43 repositories. The researchers used this benchmark to test OpenClaw, an AI agent that can perform coding tasks, and found that its performance improved significantly when paired with a specially designed adapter.

The study revealed that the design of the adapter, which helps the AI agent interact with the coding environment, is crucial for its performance. The researchers also found that the choice of AI model and harness (the system that supports the AI agent) can significantly impact the agent's accuracy and cost.

The Claw-SWE-Bench benchmark is a valuable tool for evaluating and improving AI coding assistants. It provides a comprehensive and reproducible way to compare different agents and can help researchers and developers create more effective and efficient coding tools. The data from the study is publicly available, making it a useful resource for the AI research community.
summary_short:
**Improving AI Coding Assistants: A New Benchmark for Evaluation**

Researchers have developed a new benchmark called Claw-SWE-Bench to evaluate the coding abilities of AI agents, such as OpenClaw, that can perform tasks autonomously. These agents are becoming increasingly popular, but it's challenging to measure their coding skills accurately.

The Claw-SWE-Bench benchmark provides a fair and standardized way to compare different AI agents on coding tasks. It consists of 350 coding challenges across 8 languages and 43 repositories. The researchers used this benchmark to test OpenClaw, an AI agent that can perform coding tasks, and found that its performance improved significantly when paired with a specially designed adapter.

The study revealed that the design of the adapter, which helps the AI agent interact with the coding environment, is crucial for its performance. The researchers also found that the choice of AI model and harness (the system that supports the AI agent) can significantly impact the agent's accuracy and cost.

The Claw-SWE-Bench benchmark is a valuable tool for evaluating and improving AI coding assistants. It provides a comprehensive and reproducible way to compare different agents and can help researchers and developers create more effective and efficient coding tools. The data from the study is publicly available, making it a useful resource for the AI research community.

title: ALIGNBEAM : Inference-Time Alignment Transfer via Cross-Vocabulary Logit Mixing
authors: Chirag Chawla, Pratinav Seth, Vinay Kumar Sankarapu
summary:
Here's a summary of the research paper for a general audience:

**Making AI Models Safer**

Large language models (LLMs) are powerful tools that can generate human-like text. However, they can also be vulnerable to producing harmful or unsafe content. When these models are fine-tuned for specific tasks, they can become more susceptible to generating problematic text.

Researchers have proposed various methods to prevent this, but most of them require the models to have similar "vocabularies" or ways of understanding language. This limits their effectiveness, especially when dealing with specialized models that use different vocabularies.

The ALIGNBEAM method, introduced in this paper, offers a solution. It's a simple, training-free approach that allows researchers to "translate" safety information from one model to another, even if they have different vocabularies. This is done by mixing the outputs of a safe model with a specialized model, and then selecting the safest response.

The results show that ALIGNBEAM is effective in reducing the production of harmful content while maintaining the accuracy of the specialized model. This method can be used at deployment time, without requiring any changes to the models themselves. Overall, ALIGNBEAM provides a promising solution for making AI models safer and more reliable.

title: Adjoint Method versus Physics-Informed Neural Networks in PDE-Constrained Inverse Problems
authors: Zhen Zhang, Alessandro Alla, George Em Karniadakis
summary:
**Solving Complex Problems with Math and Machine Learning**

Imagine trying to figure out what's happening inside a complex system, like a flowing river or a chemical reaction. Scientists often use math equations to model these systems, but sometimes they need to solve "inverse problems" - essentially, working backwards to identify unknown properties or patterns.

Two popular methods for solving these problems are:

1. **Adjoint Method**: A traditional approach that uses mathematical optimization to find the best solution. It's like using a map to navigate through a complex landscape.
2. **Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs)**: A newer approach that uses machine learning to solve the problem. It's like training a computer to recognize patterns in the data.

Researchers recently compared these two methods on a level playing field, using the same math equations, data, and computational resources. They found that:

* The choice of method depends on how the unknown properties are represented. If they're represented as grid-based fields, the adjoint method is often better. If they're represented as neural networks, PINNs are a natural fit.
* For time-dependent problems, PINNs can provide good solutions at a lower cost than the adjoint method.
* Combining the two methods can lead to even better results, with PINNs helping to initialize the adjoint method and improve its accuracy.

Overall, this study helps us understand the strengths and weaknesses of each approach, and how to choose the best method for a particular problem. This can lead to breakthroughs in fields like engineering, physics, and chemistry, where solving complex inverse problems is crucial.

title: Fourier Features Let Agents Learn High Precision Policies with Imitation Learning
authors: Balázs Gyenes, Emiliyan Gospodinov, Jan Frieling, Enrico Krohmer, Nicolas Schreiber, Xiaogang Jia, Niklas Freymuth, Gerhard Neumann
summary:
**Improving Robot Learning with a New Mathematical Trick**

Imagine teaching a robot to perform delicate tasks, like picking up a small object or assembling a piece of furniture. This requires the robot to understand the precise location and shape of objects in 3D space. However, robots that rely solely on 2D images often struggle with tasks that require fine-grained spatial reasoning.

Researchers have proposed using 3D point clouds, which provide a more detailed representation of the environment. However, the performance of these 3D-based methods can vary greatly depending on the task. The researchers hypothesized that this variability might be due to the way neural networks process information, which tends to focus on low-frequency features and overlook important details.

To address this issue, the researchers proposed a simple yet effective solution: mapping 3D point clouds into a high-dimensional "Fourier space". This allows the robot to access high-frequency features that are crucial for precise tasks. The researchers tested this approach on several challenging robotic manipulation tasks and found that it significantly improved the performance of various robot learning models.

The results show that this mathematical trick, called Fourier features, enables robots to leverage geometric details more effectively than traditional methods. This approach has the potential to become a general-purpose tool for robot learning, allowing robots to perform complex tasks with higher precision and accuracy. The researchers have made their code and results publicly available, which could lead to further advancements in robot learning and manipulation.

title: Measuring Semantic Progress in Multi-turn Dialogue via Information Gain
authors: Paul He, Shiva Kasiviswanathan, Dominik Janzing
summary:
**Measuring Progress in Conversations: A New Approach**

Evaluating the quality of multi-turn dialogues, like conversations between humans, is a challenging task. Researchers have proposed a new method to measure progress in these conversations, focusing on the accumulation of new and relevant information. This concept is called "semantic progress."

The researchers developed a mathematical metric that estimates semantic progress by tracking how much uncertainty is reduced as a conversation unfolds. This approach uses a simple and efficient formula that can be computed quickly, even on standard computers.

**Key Findings:**

* The proposed metric can effectively capture semantic progress in conversations, aligning well with human judgments.
* Unlike other methods that rely on large language models, this approach is lightweight and doesn't require significant computational resources.
* The method is reproducible and doesn't rely on complex autoregressive inference.

**Implications:**

* This research provides a new tool for evaluating the quality of conversations, which can be useful for improving chatbots, virtual assistants, and other dialogue systems.
* The approach can help developers create more effective and engaging conversational systems that provide valuable information to users.

Overall, this study presents a promising new approach to measuring semantic progress in conversations, with potential applications in natural language processing and human-computer interaction.

title: Harness In-Context Operator Learning with Chain of Operators
authors: Minghui Yang, Ling Guo, Liu Yang
summary:
**Breakthrough in Artificial Intelligence: A New Method for Improving Operator Learning**

Imagine you have a computer model that can learn to perform complex tasks, like simulating the flow of fluids or optimizing traffic patterns. However, these models often struggle to adapt to new, unfamiliar tasks without extensive retraining. Researchers have now developed a novel approach called Chain of Operators (CHOP) to overcome this limitation.

CHOP builds on a existing model called In-Context Operator Networks (ICON), which can learn specific tasks from examples. However, ICON sometimes fails when faced with tasks that are very different from what it was trained on. CHOP addresses this issue by creating a "chain" of simple, interpretable transformations that help the ICON model adapt to new tasks without needing to be retrained.

In tests, CHOP has shown promising results, reducing errors by leveraging a series of elementary transformations and the ICON model. Notably, this approach not only improves accuracy but also provides insights into the inner workings of the model, as each step in the chain can be understood and expressed in simple mathematical terms.

The implications of this research are significant, as it could enable the development of more versatile and efficient AI models for complex tasks in fields like physics, engineering, and optimization. By harnessing the power of CHOP, researchers and practitioners can create more adaptable and generalizable models that can tackle a wide range of challenges.

title: Learning What to Say to Your VLA: Mostly Harmless Vision Language Action Model Steering
authors: Hyun Joe Jeong, Gokul Swamy, Andrea Bajcsy
summary:
**Improving Robot Control with Smarter Language Instructions**

Imagine controlling a robot with simple voice commands, like "pick up the book." But what if the robot doesn't quite understand what you mean, or does something entirely different? Researchers have been working on Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, which allow robots to follow natural language instructions. However, these models can be finicky and don't always produce the desired results.

A new approach aims to fix this problem by teaching the robot to learn from its mistakes and improve its performance over time. The researchers developed a framework that:

1. Helps the robot find better language instructions to achieve a task
2. Creates a feedback system that adjusts the instructions in real-time
3. Predicts when the new instructions might not work and prevents them from causing harm

The best part? This approach works with existing VLA models, without requiring any changes or fine-tuning. In tests, the new system improved robot performance by 24.7% in simulations and 65.0% in real-world hardware experiments. It also ensured that the robot's actions were safe and effective, even in unexpected situations.

This research has the potential to make robots more reliable and easier to control with voice commands, which could have significant implications for areas like healthcare, manufacturing, and everyday life.

title: The Standard Interpretable Model: A general theory of interpretable machine learning to deductively design interpretable methods using Lagrangian mechanics
authors: Pietro Barbiero, Giovanni De Felice, Mateo Espinosa Zarlenga, Francesco Giannini, Filippo Bonchi, Mateja Jamnik, Giuseppe Marra, Ruggero Noris
summary:
**Breakthrough in Making AI Models More Transparent**

As AI models become increasingly complex, it's becoming harder to understand how they make decisions. To address this issue, researchers have introduced a new theory called the Standard Interpretable Model (SIM). This theory provides a general framework for designing AI models that are more transparent and easier to understand.

The SIM is based on Lagrangian mechanics, a branch of physics that describes the motion of objects. By applying these principles to AI, the researchers created a systematic approach to developing interpretable models. This approach starts by defining what interpretability means for a specific user or application.

The SIM then uses this definition to identify the key characteristics of an interpretable model and derive constraints that shape the model's behavior. This allows researchers to either modify existing AI models to make them more transparent or design new models from scratch that are inherently interpretable.

The researchers tested the SIM and found that it can identify limitations of existing methods and suggest new research directions. The SIM also provides a foundation for teaching interpretability and could help unify the field of AI research.

In simple terms, the SIM is a new tool that helps create AI models that are more transparent, explainable, and trustworthy. This breakthrough has the potential to make AI more reliable and accountable, which is essential for applications in areas like healthcare, finance, and education.

title: PianoKontext: Expressive Performance Rendering from Deadpan Context
authors: Dmitrii Gavrilev
summary:
Here's a summary of the research paper for a general audience:

**Making Piano Music More Expressive**

Imagine listening to a piano recording that sounds robotic and lacks feeling. Researchers have been working on a way to make piano music more expressive and natural-sounding. A new model called PianoKontext has been developed to achieve this goal.

PianoKontext is a computer program that takes a piano score (a list of notes) and generates a performance that sounds like a real person playing the piano. The twist is that it starts with a "deadpan" version of the music, which is a robotic and emotionless rendition. The program then uses a technique called Dynamic Time Warping to analyze the differences between the deadpan version and expressive performances.

By learning from these differences, PianoKontext can generate new performances that are more expressive and natural-sounding. This is a significant improvement over previous models, which could only work with music samples of the same length.

The researchers behind PianoKontext believe that their model has the potential to revolutionize the way we experience piano music. You can hear examples of PianoKontext in action on their demo page.

**In simple terms:** PianoKontext is a computer program that makes piano music sound more expressive and natural by learning from the differences between robotic and human performances.

title: Reroute, Don't Remove: Recoverable Visual Token Routing for Vision-Language Models
authors: Cheng-Yu Yang, Shao-Yuan Lo, Yu-Lun Liu
summary:
**Improving Efficiency in Vision-Language Models**

Vision-language models (VLMs) are a type of artificial intelligence that combines visual and textual information to understand and generate human-like responses. However, these models can be computationally expensive and require a lot of memory. One way to address this issue is to reduce the number of "visual tokens" that the model uses to process images. Visual tokens are like small pieces of information that the model extracts from images to help it understand their content.

Current methods for reducing visual tokens work by ranking them in order of importance and then permanently removing the less important ones. However, this approach can be problematic because the importance of visual tokens can change as the model processes more information. For example, a token that seems unimportant at first may become crucial later on, especially when the model is trying to understand specific queries or questions.

To address this issue, researchers have proposed a new method called "Reroute." Reroute replaces the permanent removal of visual tokens with a recoverable routing system. This means that instead of throwing away less important tokens, the model temporarily sets them aside and allows them to re-enter the processing pipeline later on. This approach allows the model to make more efficient use of its resources while still maintaining its performance.

The researchers tested Reroute on several different VLMs and found that it improved the model's ability to understand and respond to queries, especially when the model was heavily reducing the number of visual tokens. The results suggest that Reroute is a promising approach for improving the efficiency of VLMs without sacrificing their performance. The code for Reroute is publicly available, making it easy for other researchers to build on this work.

title: How Seemingly Inconsequential Design Choices Dictate Performance of LLMs in Pathology
authors: Kian R. Weihrauch, Thomas A. Buckley, William Lotter, Arjun K. Manrai
summary:
**The Surprising Impact of Design Choices on AI Performance in Pathology**

Large language models (LLMs) are often used as a baseline to evaluate the performance of specialized AI models in pathology, particularly when analyzing whole-slide images (WSIs) of tissue samples. However, researchers have found that the way these LLMs are used can significantly impact their performance.

In a recent study, researchers investigated how four design choices - inference mode, patch size, magnification, and patch count - affect the performance of LLMs in pathology tasks. They discovered that by simply changing these design choices, the performance of LLMs can improve dramatically.

For example, when classifying cancer types, the performance of GPT-5, a popular LLM, increased from 15.1% to 39.5% by using a single, optimized configuration. Similarly, when classifying organs, the performance of GPT-5 improved from 38.1% to 62.9%. These gains were also observed in other models and datasets, including a fully held-out cohort.

The study's findings suggest that previous studies may have overstated the gap between specialized AI models and general-purpose LLMs in pathology. By optimizing design choices, LLMs can achieve performance comparable to specialized models without requiring domain-specific training or architectural adaptations.

This research has important implications for the development and evaluation of AI models in pathology and other fields, highlighting the need to carefully consider design choices when using LLMs.

title: DIRECT: When and Where Should You Allocate Test-Time Compute in Embodied Planners?
authors: Jadelynn Dao, Milan Ganai, Yasmina Abukhadra, Ajay Sridhar, Mozhgan Nasr Azadani, Katie Luo, Clark Barrett, Jiajun Wu, Chelsea Finn, Marco Pavone
summary:
**Smarter Robots through Smarter Resource Allocation**

Imagine a robot that can perform complex tasks on its own, like assembling furniture or cooking a meal. These robots rely on artificial intelligence (AI) models to plan their actions, but these models can be computationally expensive and slow. Researchers have found that simply throwing more computing power at these models doesn't always lead to better results. In fact, it can even increase latency, energy consumption, and costs.

To address this issue, a team of researchers has developed a new framework called DIRECT. DIRECT helps allocate computing resources more efficiently by deciding when and where to spend computing power to achieve the best results. This approach uses the robot's surroundings and task requirements to determine the optimal amount of computing power needed.

The researchers tested DIRECT on various robotic tasks, including manipulation and long-horizon planning, and found that it can match or exceed the performance of more powerful models while using up to 65% less computing power. This means that robots can perform complex tasks more efficiently, quickly, and cost-effectively.

The implications of this research are significant, as it could enable the deployment of more capable and efficient robots in various settings, from manufacturing and healthcare to homes and public spaces. By optimizing computing resource allocation, DIRECT has the potential to bring frontier-level AI capabilities to robots at a fraction of the cost.

title: VLGA: Vision-Language-Geometry-Action Models for Autonomous Driving
authors: Jin Yao, Dhruva Dixith Kurra, Tom Lampo, Zezhou Cheng, Danhua Guo, Burhan Yaman
summary:
**Breakthrough in Autonomous Driving: Introducing VLGA**

Imagine a self-driving car that can not only see and understand its surroundings but also navigate through complex 3D environments with precision. Researchers have made a significant advancement in developing autonomous driving technology with the introduction of VLGA, a Vision-Language-Geometry-Action model.

VLGA is a sophisticated AI system that combines four key components:

1. **Vision**: The car's ability to perceive its surroundings through cameras and sensors.
2. **Language**: The car's ability to understand and describe its environment in natural language.
3. **Geometry**: The car's ability to understand the 3D layout of its surroundings.
4. **Action**: The car's ability to make decisions and take actions to navigate safely.

What sets VLGA apart is its ability to reconstruct a dense 3D representation of the world around it, allowing it to make more informed decisions. This is achieved through a novel approach that uses LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) data to supervise the model's geometry understanding.

**Key Achievements:**

* VLGA outperforms existing methods in various autonomous driving scenarios, demonstrating improved safety and efficiency.
* On the nuScenes dataset, VLGA achieves a new state-of-the-art performance with the lowest collision rate and average distance error.
* On the Bench2Drive dataset, VLGA attains a driving score of 79.08, surpassing the strongest prior method.

**Impact:**

The development of VLGA marks a significant step towards creating more advanced and reliable autonomous driving systems. With its ability to accurately perceive and understand its surroundings, VLGA has the potential to improve road safety, reduce accidents, and enhance the overall driving experience.
summary_short:
**Breakthrough in Autonomous Driving: Introducing VLGA**

Imagine a self-driving car that can not only see and understand its surroundings but also navigate through complex 3D environments with precision. Researchers have made a significant advancement in developing autonomous driving technology with the introduction of VLGA, a Vision-Language-Geometry-Action model.

VLGA is a sophisticated AI system that combines four key components:

1. **Vision**: The car's ability to perceive its surroundings through cameras and sensors.
2. **Language**: The car's ability to understand and describe its environment in natural language.
3. **Geometry**: The car's ability to understand the 3D layout of its surroundings.
4. **Action**: The car's ability to make decisions and take actions to navigate safely.

What sets VLGA apart is its ability to reconstruct a dense 3D representation of the world around it, allowing it to make more informed decisions. This is achieved through a novel approach that uses LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) data to supervise the model's geometry understanding.

**Key Achievements:**

* VLGA outperforms existing methods in various autonomous driving scenarios, demonstrating improved safety and efficiency.
* On the nuScenes dataset, VLGA achieves a new state-of-the-art performance with the lowest collision rate and average distance error.
* On the Bench2Drive dataset, VLGA attains a driving score of 79.08, surpassing the strongest prior method.

**Impact:**

The development of VLGA marks a significant step towards creating more advanced and reliable autonomous driving systems. With its ability to accurately perceive and understand its surroundings, VLGA has the potential to improve road safety, reduce accidents, and enhance the overall driving experience.

title: Illumination-Robust Camera-Based Heart-Rate Estimation for Physiological Sensing in Robots
authors: Zhi Wei Xu, Torbjörn E. M. Nordling
summary:
**Breakthrough in Heart Rate Estimation for Robots**

Imagine a robot that can monitor your heart rate while interacting with you. This technology, called remote photoplethysmography (rPPG), uses a camera to estimate heart rate without physical contact. However, changes in lighting conditions have made it challenging to accurately measure heart rate.

Researchers have now developed a new framework that enables robust heart rate estimation from a camera, even in varying lighting conditions. This end-to-end spatial-temporal transformer framework integrates several innovative techniques to improve accuracy.

**Key Findings:**

* The new framework achieves a highly accurate heart rate estimation with a mean absolute error of 0.79 beats per minute (bpm).
* This represents a 93.6% improvement over existing methods.
* The technology also shows a strong correlation of 0.982 between estimated and actual heart rates.

**Implications:**

This breakthrough has significant implications for service, social, and assistive robots that interact with humans in everyday environments. With this technology, robots can now monitor vital signs, such as heart rate, while providing assistance or companionship. This can enhance the overall human-robot interaction experience and open up new possibilities for healthcare and social robotics applications.

title: Semantically-Aware Diver Activity Recognition Framework for Effective Underwater Multi-Human-Robot Collaboration
authors: Sadman Sakib Enan, Junaed Sattar
summary:
**Breakthrough in Underwater Robotics: A New Framework for Human-Robot Collaboration**

Imagine a future where humans and robots work together seamlessly underwater, like a well-oiled machine. A new research paper proposes a significant step towards making this vision a reality. The authors have developed a novel framework called DAR-Net, which enables autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) to recognize and respond to a diver's activities, ensuring safe and effective collaboration.

The challenge lies in the underwater environment, where visibility is low and conditions are harsh. To overcome this, the researchers created a new dataset, the Underwater Diver Activity (UDA) dataset, which contains over 2,600 annotated images. This dataset is a crucial resource for training and testing AUVs to recognize diver activities.

The DAR-Net framework uses a transformer-based approach, which analyzes complex underwater scenes and classifies diver activities with high accuracy. The system was tested in a controlled environment and outperformed existing state-of-the-art models in recognizing six distinct diver activities.

This breakthrough has the potential to revolutionize underwater operations, such as search and rescue, inspection, and exploration. By enabling AUVs to understand and respond to diver activities, we can create more intelligent, collaborative, and safer underwater robotic systems. This research lays the groundwork for future studies and has significant implications for various industries, including ocean exploration, offshore energy, and marine conservation.

title: A Turbo-Inference Strategy for Object Detection and Instance Segmentation
authors: Zhen Zhao, Gang Zhang, Xiaolin Hu, Liang Tang
summary:
**Improving Object Detection and Instance Segmentation with a New Turbo-Inference Strategy**

Researchers have developed a new approach to improve object detection and instance segmentation, two important tasks in computer vision. Object detection involves locating objects within an image, while instance segmentation involves identifying the exact shape and boundaries of each object.

The traditional approach to instance segmentation involves a two-step process: detecting objects with bounding boxes and then segmenting the objects within those boxes. However, this approach can be limited by the accuracy of the initial detection step.

The new turbo-inference strategy takes a different approach. It uses two modules, turbo-detection and turbo-segmentation, which work together in a closed loop to iteratively refine the detection and segmentation results. This allows the two tasks to communicate with each other and improve each other's accuracy.

**Key Findings:**

* The turbo-inference strategy substantially enhances both detection and segmentation accuracies on several benchmark datasets, including COCO, iFLYTEK, and Cityscapes.
* The method achieves a tradeoff between prediction accuracy and inference speed, with a certain increase in computational cost.

**Implications:**

* The new approach has the potential to improve the accuracy of object detection and instance segmentation in a wide range of applications, from self-driving cars to medical imaging.
* The code for the turbo-inference strategy is publicly available, making it easy for researchers and developers to implement and build upon.

Overall, the turbo-inference strategy represents a significant advance in the field of computer vision, and has the potential to improve the accuracy and efficiency of object detection and instance segmentation tasks.

title: DepthMaster: Unified Monocular Depth Estimation for Perspective and Panoramic Images
authors: Pengfei Wang, Shihao Wang, Liyi Chen, Zhiyuan Ma, Guowen Zhang, Lei Zhang
summary:
**Breakthrough in Depth Estimation: A Single System for All Types of Images**

Imagine being able to accurately measure the distance of objects in a photo, no matter if it's a regular picture or a 360-degree panorama. Researchers have made a significant step towards achieving this with the development of DepthMaster, a unified system for monocular depth estimation.

DepthMaster can accurately estimate the depth of images from both regular cameras and 360-degree cameras, known as panoramic cameras. Previous methods struggled to work well across different types of cameras and often required specialized networks and large amounts of training data. DepthMaster overcomes these limitations by breaking down panoramic images into smaller, overlapping patches that can be processed like regular images.

The innovative system uses a novel loss function, called Correspondence Consistency Loss (CCL), and virtual projection cameras to ensure seamless stitching of the patches. This approach allows DepthMaster to work with standard computer vision architectures, making it more efficient and adaptable.

The results are impressive: DepthMaster achieved state-of-the-art performance on 13 diverse datasets, outperforming both general and specialized methods in both perspective and panoramic domains. This breakthrough has the potential to enable a wide range of applications, from robotics and autonomous driving to augmented reality and computer vision.

**In simple terms:** DepthMaster is a new system that can accurately measure the distance of objects in any type of image, whether it's a regular photo or a 360-degree panorama. This achievement has the potential to improve various technologies, such as self-driving cars and virtual reality.

title: Atlas H&E-TME: Scalable AI-Based Tissue Profiling at Expert Pathologist-Level Accuracy
authors: Kai Standvoss, Miriam Hägele, Rosemarie Krupar, Julika Ribbat-Idel, Jennifer Altschüler, Gerrit Erdmann, Hans Pinckaers, Evelyn Ramberger, Madleen Drinkwitz, Ádám Nárai, Alexander Möllers, Katja Lingelbach, Sebastian Kons, Lukas Hönig, Recepcan Adigüzel, Joana Baião, Alberto Megina Gonzalo, Marius Teodorescu, Marie-Lisa Eich, Paolo Chetta, Shakil Merchant, Verena Aumiller, Simon Schallenberg, Andrew Norgan, Klaus-Robert Müller, Lukas Ruff, Maximilian Alber, Frederick Klauschen
summary:
**Breakthrough in Cancer Research: AI System Analyzes Tissue Samples with Unprecedented Accuracy**

Imagine a tool that can help doctors analyze tissue samples from cancer patients more accurately and efficiently. A team of researchers has developed an artificial intelligence (AI) system called Atlas H&E-TME, which can analyze tissue samples stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), a common technique used in pathology.

The AI system can examine entire tissue samples, identifying different types of cells and tissue regions, and providing over 4,500 detailed readouts per sample. This is a significant improvement over traditional methods, which are often time-consuming and rely on human pathologists' interpretations.

To validate the accuracy of Atlas H&E-TME, the researchers developed a new approach that combines expert pathologists' opinions with additional information from immunohistochemistry (IHC), a technique that uses antibodies to detect specific proteins in tissue samples. They compared the AI system's performance to that of human pathologists working with H&E-stained samples alone.

The results are impressive: Atlas H&E-TME matches or exceeds the performance of human pathologists and works consistently across a wide range of cancer types, subtypes, and technical variations. This AI system has the potential to revolutionize cancer research and treatment by providing a scalable, quantitative window into the tumor and its microenvironment.

The development of Atlas H&E-TME could lead to:

* More accurate diagnoses and personalized treatment plans
* Improved understanding of cancer biology and the tumor microenvironment
* Development of new biomarkers for cancer diagnosis and treatment monitoring
* Enhanced research capabilities in translational and clinical research

Overall, this breakthrough AI system has the potential to transform the field of pathology and improve cancer care.

title: Echoes of the Prior: A Computational Phenomenology of Forgetting
authors: Gege Gao, Bernhard Schölkopf, Andreas Geiger
summary:
**The Art of Forgetting: A New Perspective on Memory Loss**

Imagine a world where your memories start to fade, and reality becomes distorted. This is what happens when our biological memory declines, but what does it look like? A team of researchers has created an interactive art installation called "Echoes of the Prior" that aims to visualize the experience of forgetting.

Using a type of artificial intelligence called a neural network, the researchers simulated the process of memory loss by inducing a kind of "synaptic decay" - a gradual weakening of the connections between brain cells. This allowed them to model how our perception of the world changes as our memories fade.

The result is a thought-provoking and artistic representation of what it might be like to lose one's grip on reality. The researchers are not just exploring the technical aspects of memory, but also the emotional and poetic aspects of forgetting.

The installation invites viewers to interact with a digital world that gradually distorts and becomes unrecognizable, much like our experience of reality when our memories fade. By using art and technology to explore the complexities of memory, the researchers hope to inspire new perspectives on the fragility of human intelligence.

**Interactive Demo:** You can experience the installation yourself at [https://decart-4d.github.io/](https://decart-4d.github.io/).

title: Anatomically Conditioned Recurrent Refinement for Topology-Aware Circle of Willis Segmentation
authors: Juraj Perić, Marija Habijan, Dario Mužević, Irena Galić, Danilo Babin, Aleksandra Pižurica
summary:
Here's a summary of the research paper for a general audience:

**Improving Brain Blood Vessel Imaging with AI**

Researchers have developed a new artificial intelligence (AI) technique to improve the accuracy of brain blood vessel imaging. The Circle of Willis, a critical network of blood vessels at the base of the brain, is notoriously difficult to image due to its complex structure and tiny size. Current AI methods often produce "broken vessel" artifacts, which can lead to inaccurate diagnoses.

The new technique, called Anatomically Conditioned Recurrent Refinement U-Net (AC2RUNet), uses a two-part approach to segment the Circle of Willis from magnetic resonance angiography (MRA) images. The first part extracts general anatomical features, while the second part iteratively refines the image to correct topological errors.

**Key Breakthroughs:**

* Improved accuracy: AC2RUNet significantly reduces errors in vessel connectivity and shape.
* Better handling of complex topology: The technique can handle the intricate structure of the Circle of Willis, reducing "broken vessel" artifacts.

**Impact:**

This research has the potential to improve the diagnosis and treatment of brain-related diseases, such as stroke and aneurysms, by providing more accurate images of brain blood vessels. The technique could also be applied to other medical imaging applications, leading to better patient outcomes.

title: Slots, Transitions, Loops: Learning Composable World Models for ARC
authors: Gege Gao, Bernhard Schölkopf, Andreas Geiger
summary:
**Unlocking the Secrets of Visual Patterns**

Imagine you're shown a few examples of how a simple game works, and then you're asked to figure out how to play a new, unseen level. This is similar to what researchers are trying to achieve with a new artificial intelligence (AI) model called Loop-OWM. The goal is to teach AI to learn and apply visual rules, like those found in games or puzzles, by observing just a few examples.

The researchers tested their model on a challenge called ARC (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus), which involves recognizing patterns in visual scenes, such as how objects change color or shape. The Loop-OWM model works by breaking down the visual scene into its individual components, like objects and colors, and then learning how these components change from one moment to the next.

The results are promising: Loop-OWM outperformed other AI models with similar or even more complex designs. This suggests that AI can learn to recognize and apply visual patterns, not just by using language or searching for pre-programmed solutions, but also by understanding the underlying rules that govern how visual scenes change over time. This breakthrough could lead to more efficient and effective AI models that can learn and adapt to new situations.

title: From 2D Grids to 1D Tokens: Reforming Shared Representations for Multimodal Image Fusion
authors: Yuchen Xian, Yunqiu Xu, Yang He, Yi Yang
summary:
Here's a summary of the research paper for a general audience:

**Improving Multimodal Image Fusion**

Imagine combining images from different sources, like infrared and visible light, to create a single image that's more informative and detailed. This process is called multimodal image fusion. Researchers have been working on improving this technique to preserve local details and maintain a consistent overall appearance.

The current methods use 2D grids to represent images, which are great for capturing local patterns but struggle to model the bigger picture. To address this, the researchers introduced a new approach that uses a 1D token interface, similar to how language models work. This allows them to capture global patterns and appearance factors more effectively.

The innovation lies in using a pre-trained image tokenizer to create a compact 1D representation of the image, which serves as a "global carrier" for the fusion process. The researchers also developed a technique called Selective Token Editing (STE), which selectively updates a small set of important tokens to ensure the fused image looks coherent and natural.

**Key Breakthroughs:**

* Better balance between local details and global appearance
* Improved performance on four benchmark datasets
* Lightweight and efficient method that doesn't require significant changes to existing fusion models

This research has the potential to enhance various applications, such as medical imaging, surveillance, and remote sensing, where multimodal image fusion is used to combine different types of images.

title: Natural-Language Temporal Grounding in Hour-Long Videos is a Search Problem: A Benchmark and Empirical Decomposition
authors: Sukmin Seo, Geewook Kim
summary:
**Understanding Hour-Long Videos with Natural Language: A New Challenge**

Imagine trying to find a specific moment in a one-hour video based on a simple description, like "the scene where the main character says goodbye to their friend." This task, called temporal grounding, is a fundamental way to interact with long videos using natural language. However, most research on this topic has focused on short videos, leaving the challenges of hour-long videos underexplored.

**The Challenge of Searching Hour-Long Videos**

The authors of a recent study argue that the main difficulty in temporal grounding for hour-long videos is not recognizing specific events, but rather searching for the relevant parts of the video. To test this idea, they created a new benchmark called ExtremeWhenBench, which consists of 2,273 queries over 194 videos, with an average duration of 75.7 minutes and a maximum duration of 9 hours.

**Current Models Struggle with Hour-Long Videos**

The study found that current video models, which are designed to understand language and video, struggle with temporal grounding in hour-long videos. In fact, a simple model that retrieves relevant frames from the video outperforms these more complex models. The authors attribute 85% of the failures to the search process, rather than recognition.

**A New Approach: Retrieve-Then-Ground**

To overcome these challenges, the authors propose a hybrid approach that combines retrieval and grounding. This approach, called retrieve-then-ground, first retrieves relevant frames from the video and then grounds the query in those frames. This approach recovers 6.7 times more accurate results than the monolithic video model, similar to how retrieve-then-read works in open-domain question answering.

**Implications and Future Directions**

The study highlights the need for more research on temporal grounding in hour-long videos and the importance of developing models that can efficiently search and ground natural-language queries in long-form video content. The ExtremeWhenBench benchmark provides a new standard for evaluating and improving temporal grounding models, which could have significant implications for applications such as video summarization, content recommendation, and human-computer interaction.

title: Findings of the MAGMaR 2026 Shared Task
authors: Alexander Martin, Dengjia Zhang, Joel Brogan, Francis Ferraro, Jeremy Gwinnup, Reno Kriz, Teng Long, Kenton Murray, Andrew Yates, Xiang Xiang
summary:
Here's a summary of the research paper for a general audience:

**Advances in Multimodal Generation and Video Retrieval**

Researchers recently conducted a competition, called MAGMaR 2026, to develop artificial intelligence (AI) systems that can work with multiple types of data, such as text and video. The competition had two main challenges:

1. **Video Retrieval**: Given a query or question, the goal was to retrieve the most relevant video from a large collection.
2. **Generating Articles from Videos**: Given a retrieved video, the goal was to generate a written article that accurately summarizes the content of the video.

The competition attracted several teams who submitted a total of 33 systems. The results were impressive:

* For video retrieval, all 17 systems submitted outperformed a baseline system, which was the best system from the previous year's competition.
* For article generation, 16 systems were submitted, and at least one generated article from each team was considered the best by human evaluators.

These findings suggest that AI systems are making significant progress in working with multiple types of data, such as text and video, and generating high-quality content. This has potential applications in areas like news summarization, content creation, and information retrieval.
summary_short:
Here's a summary of the research paper for a general audience:

**Advances in Multimodal Generation and Video Retrieval**

Researchers recently conducted a competition, called MAGMaR 2026, to develop artificial intelligence (AI) systems that can work with multiple types of data, such as text and video. The competition had two main challenges:

1. **Video Retrieval**: Given a query or question, the goal was to retrieve the most relevant video from a large collection.
2. **Generating Articles from Videos**: Given a retrieved video, the goal was to generate a written article that accurately summarizes the content of the video.

The competition attracted several teams who submitted a total of 33 systems. The results were impressive:

* For video retrieval, all 17 systems submitted outperformed a baseline system, which was the best system from the previous year's competition.
* For article generation, 16 systems were submitted, and at least one generated article from each team was considered the best by human evaluators.

These findings suggest that AI systems are making significant progress in working with multiple types of data, such as text and video, and generating high-quality content. This has potential applications in areas like news summarization, content creation, and information retrieval.

title: Bridging the Modality Gap in Forensic Image Retrieval
authors: Ricardo González-Gazapo, Annette Morales-González, Yoanna Martínez-Díaz, Heydi Méndez-Vázquez, Milton García-Borroto
summary:
**Unlocking the Power of Image Retrieval in Forensic Investigations**

Imagine being able to quickly find a specific image from a vast collection, like a tattoo or a face, to help solve a crime. This is now a reality, thanks to a new study on forensic image retrieval. Researchers have developed a system that can efficiently search for images based on visual and textual descriptions, which can greatly aid investigators.

The system uses a sophisticated artificial intelligence (AI) model to analyze images and generate textual descriptions. It then compares these descriptions to find matches, allowing investigators to quickly identify potential evidence. The researchers tested their system on four different forensic tasks:

1. Finding a tattoo image based on another tattoo image
2. Finding a tattoo image based on a verbal description from a witness
3. Finding a tattoo image from a hand-drawn sketch
4. Finding a face image from a forensic face sketch

The results showed that combining visual and textual information (known as "multimodal fusion") leads to more accurate and reliable results, especially when the visual information is limited or of poor quality (e.g., sketches or partial images). This approach can help investigators solve crimes more efficiently and effectively.

The study demonstrates the potential of AI-powered image retrieval to support investigative workflows, particularly in cases involving tattoos, facial composites, and witness descriptions. By automating the process of finding relevant images, this technology can help law enforcement agencies save time and resources, ultimately leading to faster and more accurate investigations.

title: CellNet -- Localizing Cells using Sparse and Noisy Point Annotations
authors: Benjamin Eckhardt, Dmytro Fishman, Stuart Fawke, Andrew Curtis, Bo Fussing, Constantin Pape
summary:
Here's a summary of the research paper for a general audience:

**Accurately Counting Cells with Artificial Intelligence**

Scientists often need to count living cells in laboratory experiments, which can be a time-consuming and tedious task. Researchers at the Wellcome Sanger Institute are using computer vision technology to automate this process, which is essential for studying the human genome.

In a recent study, a team developed a new algorithm that uses artificial intelligence (AI) to count cells in microscope images. The innovative part of this approach is that it requires very little human input to train the AI model. Instead of labeling every cell in an image, researchers only need to mark a few points to help the AI learn.

The results show that this method is effective, even with limited data. By automating cell counting, scientists can focus on more important research questions and make new discoveries about the human genome. The code used in this study is now publicly available, making it easier for others to use and build upon this technology.

title: Finding Sparse Subnetworks in One Training Cycle via Progressive Magnitude-Based Pruning
authors: Romana Qureshi, Hafida Benhidour, Said Kerrache, Nahlah Aljeraisy
summary:
**Simplifying Neural Networks without Sacrificing Performance**

Neural networks are powerful tools used in many applications, but they can be large and require significant computational resources. One way to make them more efficient is to "prune" them, which means removing less important parts of the network while keeping its performance intact.

Researchers have proposed various methods to prune neural networks, but most of them require multiple rounds of training, which can be time-consuming. A new approach, called progressive magnitude-based pruning, aims to achieve the same goal in just one training cycle.

This method works by gradually removing smaller weights from the network during training, using a simple and linear schedule. The results show that this approach can achieve high accuracy on image classification tasks, such as recognizing objects in images, while reducing the network size by up to 97%.

For example, on a popular benchmark dataset (CIFAR-10), this method achieved 95.12% accuracy on a ResNet-18 network at 72.9% sparsity, outperforming existing methods. Additionally, it maintained high accuracy even at extreme sparsity levels, such as 97% sparsity on a VGG-like architecture.

Overall, this research suggests that progressive magnitude-based pruning is an effective way to simplify neural networks without sacrificing their performance, which could lead to more efficient and practical AI applications.

title: VOID: Defeating Unauthorized Mimicry in Latent Diffusion Models
authors: Chunlin Qiu, Ang Li, Tianxiao Huang, Ruilin Gan, Yunjie Ge, Shenyi Zhang, Huayi Duan, Lingchen Zhao, Chao Shen, Qian Wang
summary:
**Protecting People's Images from Unauthorized Copycats**

Imagine someone using AI to create a fake image of you without your permission. This is a growing concern with the rise of powerful image-making tools called Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs). While these models can create amazing images, they can also be used to mimic individuals without their consent.

Researchers have tried to defend against this by adding subtle changes to the images, but these changes often don't work because the AI model can "fix" them and still produce a realistic copy of the person's image. To tackle this problem, a team of researchers has developed a new defense framework called VOID.

VOID works by introducing two types of disruptions to the AI model: one that breaks the image's meaning and another that cancels out the model's attempts to restore the image. This results in a corrupted image that can't be used to mimic someone without their permission.

The good news is that VOID's disruptions are imperceptible to humans, so the protected images still look normal. In fact, tests showed that VOID provides a significant improvement in protection compared to existing defenses, making it much harder for AI models to create unauthorized copies of people's images.

**In simple terms:** VOID is a new way to protect people's images from being copied or mimicked by AI models without their permission. It works by disrupting the AI model's ability to create realistic images, while still allowing the images to look normal to humans.

title: Bridging Day and Night: Unsupervised Cross-Domain Re-Identification with Synergistic Prompt and Prototype Learning
authors: Jiyang Xu, Rui Liu, Hang Dai
summary:
**Bridging Day and Night: A New Approach to Unsupervised Cross-Domain Re-Identification**

Imagine trying to identify a person in a daytime photo and then finding them in a nighttime photo. This task is challenging because the same person looks very different in daylight versus darkness. Current methods to solve this problem require a lot of labeled data, which is time-consuming and expensive to collect.

Researchers have proposed a new approach that doesn't require any manual labels. Their method uses a combination of two techniques: prompt learning and prototype-based representation learning. The approach works in two stages:

1. **Aligning images and text**: The researchers use a vision-language model to generate text prompts that describe each image. They then align these text prompts with the visual features of the images, creating a unified semantic space.
2. **Establishing identity correspondences**: The researchers create domain-specific prototype memory banks and introduce two modules to enhance feature discriminability within each domain and establish robust identity correspondences across day and night.

The results show that this unsupervised approach achieves similar accuracy to state-of-the-art fully supervised methods, which require a lot of labeled data. This breakthrough has the potential to improve applications such as surveillance and tracking, where identifying individuals across different lighting conditions is crucial.

title: Reroute, Don't Remove: Recoverable Visual Token Routing for Vision-Language Models
authors: Cheng-Yu Yang, Shao-Yuan Lo, Yu-Lun Liu
summary:
**Improving Efficiency in Vision-Language Models**

Vision-language models (VLMs) are a type of artificial intelligence that combines visual and textual information to understand and generate human-like responses. However, these models can be computationally expensive and require a lot of memory, especially when processing images. To address this issue, researchers have been exploring ways to reduce the number of "visual tokens" that the model uses to represent images.

The traditional approach to reducing visual tokens involves ranking them in order of importance and permanently removing the least important ones. However, this approach has a limitation: it doesn't account for the fact that the importance of visual tokens can change as the model processes more information. In other words, a token that seems unimportant at one stage may become crucial later on.

To overcome this limitation, researchers have proposed a new approach called "Reroute." Instead of permanently removing visual tokens, Reroute allows the model to temporarily bypass certain tokens and then re-evaluate their importance later. This approach enables the model to recover important tokens that may have been previously discarded.

The researchers tested Reroute on several VLMs and found that it improves the model's ability to perform tasks that require grounding (i.e., understanding the relationship between visual and textual information). Moreover, Reroute achieves this improvement while maintaining the model's overall performance on other tasks.

The key takeaway from this research is that reducing visual tokens in VLMs doesn't have to be a one-way process. By allowing the model to recover important tokens, Reroute offers a more efficient and effective way to process visual and textual information. This approach has the potential to enable more efficient and accurate VLMs, which can be used in a wide range of applications, from chatbots to image captioning systems.

title: FACTR 2: Learning External Force Sensing for Commodity Robot Arms Improves Policy Learning
authors: Steven Oh, Jason Jingzhou Liu, Tony Tao, Philip Han, Kenneth Shaw, Satoshi Funabashi, Ruslan Salakhutdinov, Deepak Pathak
summary:
**Robots Get a Sense of Touch Without Breaking the Bank**

Researchers have made a significant breakthrough in robotics, enabling low-cost robot arms to sense external forces without the need for expensive dedicated force sensors. This development has the potential to greatly improve the performance of robots in tasks that require manipulation and interaction with their environment.

The researchers developed a method called Neural External Torque Estimation (NEXT), which uses machine learning to estimate external forces acting on a robot arm. NEXT can learn to do this in just one minute of training data, collected over a period of 10 minutes while the arm is moving freely.

By combining NEXT with another technique called Force-Informed Re-Sampling Training (FIRST), the researchers were able to improve the performance of robot policies (or instructions) by over 17% in complex tasks. This means that robots can now learn to perform tasks more effectively, even without expensive force sensors.

The implications of this research are significant, as it enables low-cost robots to perform tasks that require a sense of touch, such as assembly, handling delicate objects, or interacting with humans. This technology has the potential to bring robots into a wider range of applications, making them more versatile and useful in various industries.
